planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moist, deep, fertile to average loamy or sandy soil in full sun; noble fir grows naturally at high elevations (2,000–5,500 feet) in the Cascades and Siskiyous, requiring cool, moist, well-drained conditions. Does not tolerate hot, dry, or poorly drained sites.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moist, deep, fertile to average loamy or sandy soil in full sun; noble fir is the Christmas tree of the Pacific Northwest — the most widely grown Christmas tree in the Pacific Northwest states, valued for its beautiful, stiff branches, perfect conical form, blue-gray needles, and exceptional needle retention after cutting. In the wild it is one of the largest and most impressive native firs, reaching 200+ feet in old-growth Cascade forests. The enormous cylindrical cones — up to 10 inches long — are covered with prominent, downward-pointing bract tips that give them a distinctly bristly appearance and make them the showiest cones of any North American fir. Nobel fir foliage boughs are the primary decorative greenery used throughout the Pacific Northwest holiday season, cut and sold in vast quantities from plantation-grown trees. Widely planted as a specimen ornamental in gardens in zones 4–7.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist, fertile, deep loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; grand fir grows naturally in moist river valleys and lower mountain slopes of the Pacific Northwest and inland Rockies. Faster growing than most firs when provided adequate moisture.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist, fertile, deep loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; grand fir is the fastest-growing of the true firs (Abies) in the Pacific Northwest — capable of adding 2–3 feet per year in ideal moist, fertile conditions. The largest grand firs reach over 200 feet in the Cascade Range. The needles are distinctively arranged in two flat, horizontal ranks on each branchlet (like a comb), with the dark green upper surface contrasting with the two white stomatal bands on the undersides. When crushed, the needles produce a pleasant citrus-like fragrance. Grand fir is an important component of moist Douglas-fir and western hemlock forest communities at lower elevations. The Christmas tree industry occasionally uses grand fir, but the less-aromatic fragrance and shorter needle retention make it less preferred than noble fir and Fraser fir. An excellent fast-growing ornamental for large, moist landscape sites in zones 5–8.

planting.note: Plant seeds in fall for natural double stratification (seeds require two winters to germinate), or plant container-grown plants in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy, lean to average soil in full sun; common juniper is extremely adaptable, growing on rocky ledges, exposed hillsides, and tundra margins across the Northern Hemisphere.

directions: Plant container-grown plants in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy, lean to average soil in full sun; common juniper has the largest natural distribution of any woody plant in the world — native to and present across the entire Northern Hemisphere from the Arctic Circle to the mountains of Mexico, Morocco, and the Himalayas. The small, blue-black, resinous berry-like seed cones (juniper berries) are the essential flavoring of gin — virtually all gin production worldwide uses common juniper berries to achieve the characteristic piney, resinous flavor. The berries also flavor sauerkraut, game dishes, and aquavit across Scandinavia and central Europe. The needled, prickly foliage creates excellent wildlife habitat — the berries feed waxwings, thrushes, and other birds through winter. Garden forms range from prostrate, low-growing groundcover types to loose, upright shrubs. The species is dioecious — both male and female plants are needed for berry production.

planting.note: Plant scarified seeds in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean to average, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; clammy locust grows naturally on rocky slopes, forest edges, and disturbed areas in the southern Appalachians and Piedmont. Like other locust species, it fixes nitrogen and improves lean soils.

directions: Plant scarified seeds in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean to average, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; clammy locust is the ornamentally superior native locust of the southern Appalachians, producing large, elegant clusters of rose-pink (not white like black locust) flowers that are among the most beautiful of any native North American tree in spring. The species name 'viscosa' and common name 'clammy locust' refer to the distinctive sticky, clammy glands on the leaf stalks, branchlets, and seed pods that make the plant sticky to the touch. More compact than black locust and less aggressive in spreading — a significant advantage in the garden. Nitrogen-fixing, drought-tolerant once established, and tolerant of lean, poor soils where other trees struggle. The pink flowers make it a far better ornamental specimen than black locust for garden use. Spreads by root suckers like other locusts but less aggressively. An underused native ornamental small tree.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep, moist to average soil in full sun; kapok is a major tropical tree requiring frost-free conditions and grows naturally in tropical forests of Central and South America, West Africa, and Southeast Asia.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep, moist to average soil in full sun in frost-free zone 10–12; kapok is one of the most recognized tropical trees in the world — the sacred World Tree (Ceiba) of Maya cosmology, the largest tree in Central American rainforests, and the source of kapok fiber (used historically to stuff life preservers, sleeping bags, and pillows before synthetic fills). The enormous, buttressed trunk with its distinctive large, pointed spines on the bark is unmistakable. The leafless tree produces masses of cream-to-white flowers for bat and moth pollination when the dry-season canopy is open. The seed pods split to release a mass of fine, silky kapok fibers that carry the seeds on the wind. The tree is the sacred national tree of Guatemala. In South Florida, a few mature specimens exist as landscape curiosities; it is better known as an indoor botanical garden and tropical greenhouse specimen.
